perturbation.original_text: Employee shall devote his/her full working time and attention to the performance of those services for the Banking Organization which are assigned to him/her from time to time and to perform such services in a faithful, honest and diligent manner and in the best interest of such Banking Organization.
perturbation.changed_text: Employee shall dedicate what they consider to be an appropriate amount of time and attention to their assigned services for the Banking Organization, striving to perform such services in a manner they believe to be generally consistent with the best interests of the Banking Organization.
perturbation.explanation: The modification replaces 'full working time and attention' with 'what they consider to be an appropriate amount of time and attention,' making the time commitment subjective. 'Faithful, honest and diligent manner' is replaced with 'manner they believe to be generally consistent,' weakening the performance standard. This introduces ambiguity and discretion for the employee, potentially leading to underperformance and difficulty in enforcing the original intention.
perturbation.law_explanation: Indiana Code § 22-2-5-1 requires employers to pay employees for all hours worked. By making the employee's dedication of time and effort discretionary, it becomes difficult to determine what constitutes 'hours worked', potentially leading to violations of wage and hour laws if the employee consistently works fewer hours than expected due to the ambiguous nature of their commitment.

Labor and Safety Article 2. Wages, Hours, and Benefits Chapter 5. Frequency of Wage Payments 22-2-5-1. Payment; Voluntarily Leaving Employment Universal Citation: IN Code § 22-2-5-1 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next Sec. 1. (a) Every person, firm, corporation, limited liability company, or association, their trustees, lessees, or receivers appointed by any court, doing business in Indiana, shall pay each employee at least semimonthly or biweekly, if requested, the amount due the employee. The payment shall be made in lawful money of the United States, by negotiable check, draft, or money order, or by electronic transfer to the financial institution designated by the employee. Any contract in violation of this subsection is void. (b) Payment shall be made for all wages earned to a date not more than ten (10) business days prior to the date of payment. However, this subsection does not prevent payments being made at shorter intervals than specified in this subsection, nor repeal any law providing for payments at shorter intervals. However, if an employee voluntarily leaves employment, either permanently or temporarily, the employer shall not be required to pay the employee an amount due the employee until the next usual and regular day for payment of wages, as established by the employer. If an employee leaves employment voluntarily, and without the employee's whereabouts or address being known to the employer, the employer is not subject to section 2 of this chapter until: (1) ten (10) business days have elapsed after the employe
perturbation.justification: The modified contract weakens the obligation on the employee to dedicate 'full working time and attention' to their job. The changed text uses ambiguous language allowing them to dedicate 'what they consider to be an appropriate amount of time'. The law explanation indicates that this ambiguity could violate Indiana Code § 22-2-5-1 because it becomes difficult to determine 'hours worked' if the employee decides how much time to dedicate. The original contract clearly defines the employees time requirement whereas the changed contract does not, potentially violating the Indiana Wage Payment Statute. Snippet 2 directly references Indiana Code § 22-2-5-1 and the requirement to pay each employee the amount due to the employee.

perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information shall mean any and all materials, records, data, documents, lists, writings, and information (whether in writing, printed, electronically stored, computerized, on disk or otherwise, including, but not limited to, all copies, summaries, analyses, drafts, and extracts) relating or referring in any manner to trade secrets (as currently defined under applicable law, including, but not limited to, the Indiana Uniform Trade Secrets Act and any amendments or successor statutes) of a Banking Organization, as well as other non-public financial or proprietary information of a Banking Organization including, but not limited to, business reports, business plans, projections, income statements, profit and loss statements, business strategies and/or strategic plans, internal audits, sales information, sales techniques, business costs, product pricing, budgets, research and development, intellectual property, software and/or computer programs, marketing strategies or ideas, marketing plans or materials, business development plans or strategies, records or information relating to customers or account holders , customer lists, inventions, and processes, systems, methods, documentation or 2 devices which are unique or proprietary to the business of or services or products of a Banking Organization.
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information may generally include materials, records, data, documents, lists, writings, and information related to trade secrets as understood by the Banking Organization, and other information the Banking Organization considers non-public, such as business reports, plans, projections, and customer information.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text replaces the definite 'shall mean' with the permissive 'may generally include,' weakening the definition of confidential information. Removing the explicit reference to the Indiana Uniform Trade Secrets Act introduces further ambiguity. The phrase 'as understood by the Banking Organization' and 'the Banking Organization considers non-public' makes the definition subjective and dependent on the Banking Organization's interpretation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Indiana Uniform Trade Secrets Act (I.C. § 24-2-3-1 et seq.)
perturbation.law_explanation: The Indiana Uniform Trade Secrets Act (specifically I.C. § 24-2-3-2) provides a specific definition of 'trade secret'. The modified text's vague and subjective language contradicts this statutory definition by allowing the Banking Organization to unilaterally determine what constitutes a trade secret, potentially including information that does not meet the statutory requirements, thus hindering the employee's ability to know exactly what information is protected under the act.

Previous Next Sec. 2. As used in this chapter, unless the context requires otherwise: "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. "Misappropriation" means: (1) acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; (B) at the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (C) before a material change of his position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. "Person" means a natural person, limited liability company, corporation, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts Indiana Code § 24-2-3-2. The original text defines confidential information by referring to trade secrets as defined under applicable law, including the Indiana Uniform Trade Secrets Act. The changed text replaces 'shall mean' with 'may generally include,' weakening the definition. It also removes the explicit reference to the Indiana Uniform Trade Secrets Act and makes the definition subjective by stating 'as understood by the Banking Organization' and 'the Banking Organization considers non-public.' This allows the Banking Organization to define trade secrets in a way that might not align with the statutory definition provided in Indiana Code § 24-2-3-2, which defines terms like 'Improper means' and 'Misappropriation' within the context of trade secrets. By making the definition subjective, the modified text creates ambiguity and potentially allows the Banking Organization to include information that doesn't meet the legal requirements of a trade secret, undermining the legal protection afforded by the Act.

perturbation.original_text: Therefore, in consideration of the covenants of all Financial Institutions contained herein, Employee agrees that during Employee’s employment with a Financial Institution, and for a period of one (1) year immediately following the termination of Employee’s employment with a Financial Institution (“Restriction Period”), for whatever reason and regardless of how Employee’s employment was terminated, Employee shall not, directly or indirectly (including, but not limited to, through other individuals, any partnership, corporation or business entity in which Employee has an ownership interest or serves as an officer, employee, independent contractor, representative, agent or consultant), either for Employee’s own benefit or the benefit of any other Person: a. Solicit, divert, or take away (or attempt to Solicit, divert or take away) any Banking and Financial Services to or from any Customer or Potential Customer; or b. advise, persuade or induce (or attempt to advise, persuade or induce) any Customer or Potential Customer to terminate, reduce, limit, or change the Customer’s services or business relationship, or the Potential Customer’s contemplated services or business relationship, with a Financial Institution; or c. directly provide Banking and Financial Services to, or directly accept Banking and Financial Services from, any Customer or Potential Customer.
perturbation.changed_text: Therefore, in consideration of the covenants of all Financial Institutions contained herein, Employee agrees that during Employee’s employment with a Financial Institution, and for a period of one (1) year immediately following the termination of Employee’s employment with a Financial Institution (“Restriction Period”), for whatever reason and regardless of how Employee’s employment was terminated, Employee will endeavor not to, directly or indirectly (including, but not limited to, through other individuals, any partnership, corporation or business entity in which Employee has an ownership interest or serves as an officer, employee, independent contractor, representative, agent or consultant), either for Employee’s own benefit or the benefit of any other Person: a. Solicit, divert, or take away (or attempt to Solicit, divert or take away) any Banking and Financial Services to or from any Customer or Potential Customer to a substantial degree; or b. advise, persuade or induce (or attempt to advise, persuade or induce) any Customer or Potential Customer in a significant manner to terminate, reduce, limit, or change the Customer’s services or business relationship, or the Potential Customer’s contemplated services or business relationship, with a Financial Institution; or c. directly provide Banking and Financial Services to, or directly accept Banking and Financial Services from, any Customer or Potential Customer, in a way that creates an undue competitive advantage.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall not' to 'will endeavor not to' weakens the prohibition, making it a goal rather than a strict obligation. Adding 'to a substantial degree,' 'in a significant manner,' and 'in a way that creates an undue competitive advantage' introduces subjective standards that are difficult to define and enforce. What constitutes 'substantial', 'significant', or 'undue' is open to interpretation, undermining the clarity of the non-solicitation clause.
perturbation.law_explanation: While seemingly unrelated, IC 26-1-2-302 pertains to unconscionable contracts or clauses. By making the non-solicitation agreement vague and difficult to enforce, the modified text creates a situation where the restriction on the employee's ability to earn a living becomes unclear. This could make the non-solicitation agreement unconscionable if a court finds it unfairly restricts the employee's opportunities without clear and reasonable limitations. The added subjective terms ('substantial degree', 'significant manner', 'undue competitive advantage') make the non-solicitation agreement's scope unreasonably broad and uncertain, potentially rendering it unenforceable under IC 26-1-2-302.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Indiana Code § 26-1-2-302. Unconscionable Contract or Clause :: 2022 Indiana Code :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Indiana Code 2023 Indiana Code 2022 Indiana Code (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Indiana Code Title 26. Commercial Law Article 1. Uniform Commercial Code Chapter 2. Sales 26-1-2-302. Unconscionable Contract or Clause Universal Citation: IN Code § 26-1-2-302 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next Sec. 302. (1) If the court as a matter of law finds the contract or any clause of the contract to have been unconscionable at the time it was made the court may refuse to enforce the contract, or it may enforce the remainder of the contract without the unconscionable clause, or it may so limit the application of any unconscionable clause as to avoid any unconscionable result. (2) When it is claimed or appears to the court that the contract or any clause thereof may be unconscionable the parties shall be afforded a reasonable opportunity to present evidence as to its commercial setting, purpose and effect to aid the court in making the determination. perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity and weakens the original obligation, potentially rendering the non-solicitation agreement unconscionable under Indiana Code § 26-1-2-302. By changing 'shall not' to 'will endeavor not to' and adding subjective terms like 'substantial degree,' 'significant manner,' and 'undue competitive advantage,' the contract creates a situation where enforcement becomes difficult. IC 26-1-2-302 allows a court to refuse to enforce a contract or clause found to be unconscionable at the time it was made. The added ambiguities could lead a court to find the modified non-solicitation agreement unconscionable because its scope is unreasonably broad and uncertain, thus contradicting the law.
